Modules
Curriculum → Data structures and algorithms interview prep → Company-specific DSA rounds → Amazon online assessment practice set

Lesson 1 of 3

Amazon online assessment practice set

Work through Amazon-style arrays, strings, trees, and DP prompts with an emphasis on clarity and correctness under time pressure.

30-45 min DSA flow Company-specific DSA rounds 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Amazon screens frequently reward clean implementation, pragmatic edge-case handling, and quick selection of a standard pattern without overengineering.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Pattern lens Name the core data structure choice before you code.2. Practice set Two Sum · Amazon · Arrays and Strings · Editor's choice: Frequently asked in an Amazon online assessment.3. Execution focus Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Amazon screens frequently reward clean implementation, pragmatic edge-case handling, and quick selection of a standard pattern without overengineering. 2. Pattern lens Name the core data structure choice before you code.3. Practice set Two Sum · Amazon · Arrays and Strings · Editor's choice: Frequently asked in an Amazon online assessment.4. Execution focus Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Amazon screens frequently reward clean implementation, pragmatic edge-case handling, and quick selection of a standard pattern without overengineering.

What to say first

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
  • Narrate the invariant that keeps the solution correct.
2

Study section

Pattern lens

Use this set to practice arriving at a workable solution quickly, then tightening it once the main data structure and invariant are correct.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Name the core data structure choice before you code.
  • State the target time and space complexity up front.
  • Use one invariant or pointer relationship to guide the implementation.
3

Study section

Practice set

Use this cluster to rehearse the same solving pattern across multiple prompts. Start with Two Sum, Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters, String to Integer (atoi) and keep your explanation focused on why the pattern applies.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Two Sum · Amazon · Arrays and Strings · Editor's choice: Frequently asked in an Amazon online assessment.
  • Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters · Amazon · Arrays and Strings
  • String to Integer (atoi) · Amazon · Arrays and Strings
  • Container With Most Water · Amazon · Arrays and Strings
  • Integer to Roman · Amazon · Arrays and Strings
  • Roman to Integer · Amazon · Arrays and Strings
4

Study section

Execution focus

Pretend you are in an OA or early technical screen: finish the first correct version, test the main edge cases, then improve only if time remains.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Walk one example manually before finalizing the code.
  • Call out the edge case that is most likely to break the first draft.
  • Finish by restating the complexity and one possible follow-up optimization.

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Clarify constraints, input shape, and expected output before coding.
  • Choose the intended pattern and defend its complexity.
  • Narrate the invariant that keeps the solution correct.
  • Test at least one edge case and one representative happy path aloud.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Jumping into code without naming the dominant pattern or invariant.
  • Optimizing too early instead of getting the first correct version working.
  • Ignoring duplicates, empty inputs, or off-by-one boundaries until the end.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • What is the fastest correct baseline you would code first under a timed assessment?
  • Which follow-up optimization would you mention only after the initial solution is stable?
  • How do you keep your code readable when the timer is the main pressure?

Coding practice

DSA interview workspace

This lab only surfaces runnable DSA prompts from the lesson set and keeps the editor in a single-file interview format.

WASM runtime idle 6 runnable questions

Problem statement

Two Sum

Amazon Arrays and Strings

Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target.

You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice.

You can return the answer in any order.

 

Example 1:

Input: nums = [2,7,11,15], target = 9
Output: [0,1]
Output: Because nums[0] + nums[1] == 9, we return [0, 1].

Example 2:

Input: nums = [3,2,4], target = 6
Output: [1,2]

Example 3:

Input: nums = [3,3], target = 6
Output: [0,1]

 

Constraints:

  • 2 <= nums.length <= 104
  • -109 <= nums[i] <= 109
  • -109 <= target <= 109
  • Only one valid answer exists.

 

Follow-up: Can you come up with an algorithm that is less than O(n2) time complexity?

Hints

  • A really brute force way would be to search for all possible pairs of numbers but that would be too slow. Again, it's best to try out brute force solutions for just for completeness. It is from these brute force solutions that you can come up with optimizations.
  • So, if we fix one of the numbers, say <pre>x</pre>, we have to scan the entire array to find the next number <pre>y</pre> which is <pre>value - x</pre> where value is the input parameter. Can we change our array somehow so that this search becomes faster?
  • The second train of thought is, without changing the array, can we use additional space somehow? Like maybe a hash map to speed up the search?

Runtime model

Runs in-browser with the bundled Pyodide WebAssembly runtime.

Ln 1, Col 1 3 lines 10 words 0.1 KB python

Test case

Run result

Run the selected test case to compare your output against the expected result.
